mem_1774320573262_5wbio.name: Charlie

messages.content: I'd like to create a memorial for Charlie. My relationship to them: sibling. Please warmly welcome me and ask your first gentle question to begin.

Welcome, and thank you for being here. Creating something lasting for Charlie is a beautiful act of love, and I'll be with you every step of the way — we can go as slowly as you need.

To begin: what's the first word or two that comes to mind when you think of Charlie?
Charlie was my oldest brother. I looked up to him so much and am going to miss him terribly

What a tender thing to carry — looking up to someone your whole life, and now navigating the world without him. Charlie sounds like he held a really special place for you.

What was it about him that made you look up to him so much?
I am not sure what I will do. We use to spend so much time together. He taught be everything I now.

The depth of that bond is so clear — someone who didn't just love you, but shaped you. It sounds like Charlie was truly woven into who you are.

What's one of the things he taught you that you find yourself carrying with you most?
He taught me how to drive a race car but that is how he died

Thank you for trusting me with that — and I'm so sorry. There's something so deeply bittersweet about that, that one of the greatest gifts he gave you is also connected to losing him.

You don't have to go anywhere near that if you'd rather not — but if you'd like to share, what did it mean to you when he taught you to drive? What do you remember about those times together?
I remember going for burger at our favorite restaurant after. He would always order the same thing and so would I. Burger, Beers and wings

That image is so vivid and so full of him — the two of you sitting down together after a day at the track, same order every time. There's something so beautifully Charlie about that kind of ritual.

What was it like, those moments sitting across from him at that restaurant?
we use to sit beside each other so we could people watch. It usually had us laughing at something or another in no time.

That's such a wonderful picture — the two of you side by side, finding the same things funny, laughing together over burgers and wings. It sounds like Charlie had a real gift for making ordinary moments feel joyful.

You mentioned his laugh earlier — can you tell me a little more about it? What did it sound like, or what did it feel like to make him laugh?
I cant believe he is gone. This is horrible

I know. I'm so sorry. Take all the time you need — there's no rush here at all.

Whenever you're ready, we can keep going — or we can just sit with this for a moment. Whatever feels right to you.
Can you tell me a funny joke to take my mind off things

I wish I could make this easier for you. Here's one:

Why don't scientists trust atoms?

Because they make up everything. 😊

I'm here with you whenever you feel ready to continue — there's absolutely no hurry.
Thank you! I have so much to do now and dont have my brother with me for any of it. what can I do to make this better

That weight you're carrying — all the things ahead without him beside you — that's one of the hardest parts of grief. There's no quick fix, and it's okay that it feels overwhelming right now.

But I want to say this: you've already done something really meaningful today. You've kept Charlie alive in words — his laugh, your spot at the restaurant, the race track, burgers and wings and people watching together. That love doesn't go anywhere.

As a final question, and you can take as much space as you need with this one: when you imagine people reading Charlie's memorial one day — whether that's family, friends, or someone who never got to meet him — what is the one thing you most want them to know about who he was?

mem_1774320573262_5wbio.tribute_text:
Charlie was the oldest brother — steady, generous, and deeply present in the life of the one who loved him most. He was the kind of person who didn't just stand beside his family but actively shaped them, pouring his time and knowledge and laughter into the people he cared for. His sibling looked up to him not simply because he was older, but because Charlie was the sort of man who made you feel like you were learning something important just by being near him.

He had a gift for turning ordinary moments into something worth remembering. After long days together at the race track, the two of them would settle in side by side at their favorite restaurant — always the same seats, always the same order of burgers, beers, and wings — and watch the world go by until something made them laugh, which never took very long. There was a ritual tenderness to those afternoons that spoke volumes about who Charlie was: someone who found joy in the familiar, who made space for the simple pleasure of sitting close to someone you love and sharing the same view.

What Charlie left behind cannot be measured in milestones or accomplishments alone, but in the countless ways he wove himself into someone else's life. He taught his sibling how to drive, how to find humor in the everyday, and how to show up fully for the people who matter. The loss of him is enormous and real, and yet everything he gave — every lesson, every laugh, every quiet moment side by side — remains. Charlie is not only missed; he endures, in all the ways he made the people he loved who they are.

mem_1774571906429_3o3t7.name: Forbes

messages.content: I'd like to create a memorial for Forbes. My relationship to them: close friend. Please warmly welcome me and ask your first gentle question to begin.

Welcome, and thank you for being here. Creating something to honour Forbes is a really beautiful thing to do, and I'll walk alongside you gently through this — there's no rush at all.

To begin: could you tell me a little about who Forbes was as a person — maybe just the first thing that comes to mind when you think of them?
Forbes was a unique character from Beetlejuice Island, which he was highly fond of.

What a wonderful detail — his connection to Beetlejuice Island sounds like it was a real part of who he was.

What was it about Beetlejuice Island that Forbes loved so much — do you know what drew him to it, or what it meant to him?
There was lots of little green things there to see. It was his favorite colour

That's such a lovely image — Forbes surrounded by all those little green things in his favourite colour.

Did he have a particular way of engaging with the world around him — was he someone who noticed and appreciated the small details in life?
He appreciate the pub and drinking beer with his buddies

That warmth of good company and a cold beer with his mates — it sounds like Forbes really knew how to enjoy the simple pleasures in life.

Can you tell me about one of those times with his buddies — is there a particular moment or memory that stands out?
He had an act for getting people to do things, even if they didn't want to. He was definitely the leader of the pack.

That's such a vivid picture of him — the kind of person who could rally everyone together and get things moving, whether they liked it or not!

Do you have a story of a time when Forbes managed to convince you or someone else to do something you wouldn't have done without him?
Yes, one of his buddies would probably still in a lot of mud if it wasnt for Forbes.

Ha, that's brilliant — Forbes clearly had a gift for getting people out of sticky situations as much as getting them into them!

What happened — how did Forbes come to the rescue that day?
He made a great deal on his buddies behalf, saved his house that day! Something that would never be forgotten

What a moment — Forbes stepping up and going to bat for his friend like that says so much about the kind of loyalty and heart he had.

It sounds like Forbes was someone people could really count on when it mattered most. How would you describe the bond between the two of you?
We were as close as a sock and his favorite item a towel

A sock and a towel — that's such a perfect, tender way to put it, always close, always together in the everyday moments of life.

As we near the end of our time together, I'd love to ask — what do you think Forbes's lasting mark on the world was? What is it that he left behind in the people who knew him?
He donated every towel that he owned to the village. He always said everyone should have a good towel or two. Now they did

What a beautiful final act — making sure everyone in his village had something he believed in so simply and so deeply. That is Forbes through and through, isn't it? Someone who led the pack, who saved his friend's house, who loved the green of Beetlejuice Island and the warmth of good company at the pub — and who, in the end, made sure the people around him had exactly what they needed.

Thank you so much for sharing Forbes with me today. The picture you've painted of him — this big-hearted, persuasive, loyal, generous soul who measured life's richness in green islands, cold beers, good friends, and quality towels — is one that deserves to be remembered and celebrated. The world was warmer for having him in it, and it's clear he left a little piece of himself in everyone he knew, including you. What a privilege it's been to hear his story.

mem_1774571906429_3o3t7.tribute_text:
Forbes was a unique soul, a man shaped by the place he loved most — Beetlejuice Island, with all its little green things that delighted him in the way only a favourite colour truly can. He was a man who noticed the world around him, who found meaning in the small and the simple, and who carried a deep fondness for the island that felt like a part of his very identity. To know Forbes was to understand that he had an eye for what mattered, and a heart that held it all close.

He was, by every account, the leader of the pack — a man with a rare and irresistible gift for getting people to do things they never would have done on their own. Nowhere was this more apparent than the day he stepped in for one of his dearest friends, negotiating a deal that saved his buddy's house and pulled him well and truly out of the mud. Forbes knew how to show up when it counted, and that loyalty, that willingness to go to bat for the people he loved, was perhaps the most defining thing about him. He and his friends knew the warmth of good company and a cold beer at the pub, and those simple gatherings were never small things — they were the threads of a life well lived.

In the end, Forbes left behind something as practical and profound as the man himself. He donated every towel he owned to his village, because he had always believed that everyone deserved a good towel or two — and now, they did. It was so entirely Forbes: generous without ceremony, caring in the most grounded and human of ways. The bond between Forbes and those who loved him was as close and constant as a sock and a towel, and the warmth he gave so freely now lives on in every corner of the community he cherished.
